evidence_quotes.quote: specifically video-games that require a connection to a remote server provided by said games publisher.

paragraphs.text: Hi, I am a citizen of the European Union and would like to provide some feedback regarding the Digital Fairness Act: I would like to see the initiative take action on the rules and regulations regarding sale of digital licenses to customers, specifically video-games that require a connection to a remote server provided by said games publisher. At the time of writing there is noting stopping a publisher from switching off support for a video game, removing features and in some cases renering the product unusable. Consumers are not informed about any potential time-frame specifying how long their purchase will be usable and is completely up to the whim of the publisher. This does not comply with existing expectations for consumer protections that are in place for purchases of physical products or subscriptions where indeed the seller is required to be transparent about for how long your purchase will be valid. I believe we have a right as consumers to at least be informed for how long a purchase of a video game will be useable at the minimum, or preferably that publishers of video games are required to take end-of-life support into consideration when designing their products as to ensure the product that was purchased can still be used by the consumer in some capacity whenever officiall support ends. An example: A publisher should not be allowed to sue or otherwise stop attempts by consumers who purchased their product from re-enabling the games functionality themselves through modding or software development IF the official support of the video game has ended. This problem applies to any digital product that is dependent on a remote server provided by a developer/publisher of said product.
